advantages: Adapts to complex, high-dimensional patterns that are impractical to encode as hand-written rules (vision, language, user behavior).
Improves automatically as more representative training data arrives—behavior can track shifting real-world distributions.
Reuses a common workflow (data, model, loss, evaluation) across domains from tabular fraud to generative text.
Combines with probabilistic objectives (MLE) so training losses have a clear statistical interpretation.
Enables personalization and ranking at scale by learning per-user or per-context signals from logs.
disadvantages: Requires quality labeled or structured data; biased, sparse, or mislabeled data propagates into model behavior.
Black-box models can be hard to audit, debug, and explain compared to explicit rule systems.
Risk of overfitting—memorizing training noise instead of learning patterns that generalize to new inputs.
Training and serving infrastructure (compute, monitoring, retraining) adds operational cost beyond static software.
No guarantee of correctness or fairness; models reflect statistical patterns in data, including harmful ones.
